source: Cattle Medicine - Scott, Penny, Macrae (Manson Publishing, 2011)
disclaimer: This data is extracted from a veterinary reference textbook for informational guidance only. Always consult a certified veterinarian before administering any medication.

medications.frequency: q24h or q12h depending on condition
medications.notes: Higher doses (up to 300,000 iu/kg) recommended on first day for listerial encephalitis to exceed MIC by 10-30 times. Standard dose 44,000 iu/kg for most conditions.
Given IV first for rapid onset, followed by procaine penicillin IM. For tetanus: 50 units/kg IV then IM doses as needed.

medications.frequency: repeat within 4-6 hours, then twice daily for 3 more days
medications.notes: Improvement usually evident within 24 hours if given early. Full recovery may take one week.
Use at first presentation. Controversial for meningoencephalitis but reported to improve survival. Also used to reduce perivulval oedema post-dystocia.
Used alongside thiamine therapy. Slow IV drip essential.

medications.frequency: 2 occasions, 10-14 days apart (leptospirosis); 5-7 days parenteral for wooden tongue
medications.duration: 2 doses for leptospirosis; 5-7 days for wooden tongue
medications.notes: Often used as penicillin-streptomycin combination preparation for wooden tongue. For leptospirosis: 25 mg/kg on 2 occasions 10-14 days apart.
Do NOT use in pregnant animals. Stop if signs of iodism appear (lacrimation, nasal discharge, skin scaling). In some countries (e.g. EU) may affect food chain eligibility.

medications.frequency: can repeat every 2-3 days until signs of iodism
medications.notes: Very irritant if it leaks perivascularly - must be given via catheter. Do NOT use in pregnant animals.
Used alongside NSAID (flunixin or ketoprofen) for superfoul. Also used topically/systemically for granular vulvitis.

medications.frequency: q8h for tetanus; single dose for sedation
medications.notes: No analgesic properties. For tetanus: 0.05 mg/kg q8h for muscle relaxation. For caesarean sedation: 5-8 mg for a 600 kg cow IV.

medications.max_dose: 3 mg/kg (8-10 ml of 200 mg/ml solution for adult cow)
medications.notes: Sedative of choice for hypomagnesaemia seizures. 10 ml bolus of 20% solution used for calf euthanasia in hip lock - has no effect on the cow.

medications.min_dose: 400 ml of 25% solution (subcutaneous, after IV calcium)
medications.route: subcutaneous injection (after IV calcium borogluconate); oral drench for lead
medications.frequency: single dose; repeat dietary supplementation to prevent relapse
medications.duration: injection lasts only 6-12 hours - dietary follow-up essential
medications.notes: For hypomagnesaemia: give 400 ml of 40% calcium borogluconate IV first, then 400 ml of 25% magnesium sulphate SC. For lead: oral drench 500-1000 g to precipitate lead from gut.
For hypomagnesaemia: administer IV before subcutaneous magnesium sulphate. For LDA supportive care: 400 ml IV.

medications.min_dose: standard label dose (single long-acting injection available)
medications.route: injection (single long-acting formulation or days 1 and 3)
medications.frequency: single long-acting injection or days 1 and 3
medications.notes: Preferred for TEME as it has lowest MIC against H. somni. Highly effective against major respiratory pathogens. Long-acting formulations available.

medications.route: intravenous (day 1 for metritis); injection for respiratory
medications.frequency: 3 consecutive days for metritis; as per label for respiratory
medications.notes: 75-80% of lung pasteurellae isolates are sensitive. Long-acting formulations available for respiratory disease. Combined with NSAID (ketoprofen or flunixin) for metritis.
Has ecbolic effect - may make uterus more friable. Affords no analgesia at sedation doses. Cattle can still kick when apparently sedated.

For caesarean: 3-5 mg/kg as line or inverted-L block (typically 100-150 ml of 2% solution). For extradural: 5-7 ml at sacrococcygeal site. For digit: 25-30 ml of 2% solution for IV regional anaesthesia.
